title: First Conversations
description: Time to use it for real. These are the everyday situations you'll actually face in Germany — buying a ticket, ordering food, finding your way. Learn the exact phrases that get the job done.

lessons.title: At the Train Station
lessons.subtitle: Buy a ticket, find your platform, ask when the train leaves.
lessons.hook: The Bahnhof (train station) is often a newcomer's first real German test. The good news: ticket conversations are short and follow a script. Learn four or five phrases and you can get a ticket to anywhere in Germany with confidence — no panic, no pointing.
lessons.explain: In a station you mainly need to do three things: ask for a ticket, ask about the platform (das Gleis), and ask about times. Key phrases: 'Eine Fahrkarte nach ___, bitte' (A ticket to ___, please), 'Wann fährt der Zug?' (When does the train leave?), and 'Von welchem Gleis?' (From which platform?). Germans appreciate 'bitte' (please) and 'danke' (thank you) — politeness is expected, not optional. Keep sentences short; you don't need to be fancy to be understood.

examples.german: Wann fährt der Zug nach Hamburg?
examples.english: When does the train to Hamburg leave?

examples.german: Von welchem Gleis fährt der Zug?
examples.english: From which platform does the train leave?

vocabulary.memoryHook: Fahr (drive) + Karte (card) = a card for driving/travelling.
Bahn (rail) + Hof (yard) = the rail yard. 'Hauptbahnhof' = main station.
das Gleis — listen for 'Gleis' + a number in announcements.

vocabulary.exampleSentence: Der Zug fährt um 9 Uhr ab.
vocabulary.memoryHook: ab (off/away) + fahren. Separable: '...fährt ... ab' splits apart.
-ung ending → die. Sadly, a word you'll use often with German trains.

exercises.prompt: You want a ticket to Cologne (Köln). What do you say?
exercises.explanation: 'Eine Fahrkarte nach [city], bitte' is the standard, polite way to request a ticket. 'nach' is used for destinations (cities and countries).
das Gleis is the platform/track. When you hear 'Gleis 5', your train is at track 5. Don't confuse it with die Fahrkarte (ticket) or der Zug (train).

exercises.prompt: How do you ask 'When does the train leave?'
exercises.explanation: 'Wann' means when. 'Wo' = where, 'Was' = what, 'Wer' = who. So 'Wann fährt der Zug?' asks about the time of departure.

lessons.title: Ordering Food
lessons.subtitle: Order a coffee, a meal, and ask for the bill — like a local.
lessons.hook: Cafés and restaurants are where German gets fun and low-stakes. Nobody expects perfection — they expect a friendly 'Ich möchte...' (I would like...). Master that one polite phrase and you can order almost anything on any menu in the country.
lessons.explain: The magic phrase for ordering is 'Ich möchte...' (I would like...) — it's polite and works everywhere. Add the item: 'Ich möchte einen Kaffee' (I would like a coffee). To ask the price: 'Was kostet das?' (What does that cost?). To pay: 'Die Rechnung, bitte' (The bill, please) or 'Zahlen, bitte' (To pay, please). Note the Accusative from Chapter 4 in action: einen Kaffee (masculine → einen). Tipping is normal but modest — you often round up and say the total you want to pay.

examples.english: I would like a coffee, please.

vocabulary.memoryHook: The politeness verb. ich möchte, du möchtest, er möchte. Safer than 'ich will' (I want).
der Kaffee → 'einen Kaffee' when you order (Accusative masculine).
das Wasser stays 'ein Wasser' in the Accusative (neuter never changes).
Sounds like English 'cost'. 'Was kostet das?' — your price-check phrase.

exercises.prompt: What is the polite way to order 'a coffee'?
exercises.explanation: 'Ich möchte... bitte' is polite and expected. Notice 'einen Kaffee' — der Kaffee is masculine, and as the thing being ordered (the object) it becomes einen (Accusative).

exercises.prompt: How do you ask for the bill?
exercises.explanation: die Rechnung = the bill. 'Die Rechnung, bitte' or 'Zahlen, bitte' both work when you're ready to pay.

exercises.prompt: Order a water: 'Ich möchte ___ Wasser, bitte.' (das Wasser is neuter)
exercises.hint: Neuter indefinite article — and neuter doesn't change in the Accusative.
exercises.explanation: das Wasser is neuter → ein Wasser. Neuter 'ein' is identical in Nominative and Accusative, so it stays 'ein' even though the water is the object being ordered.

lessons.title: Asking for Directions
lessons.subtitle: Find the station, the toilet, the way — and understand the answer.
lessons.hook: Getting lost is the fastest way to learn a city — and directions are one of the most reusable conversations in any language. The core is a single question word: 'Wo?' (Where?). 'Wo ist...?' plus a place gets you 90% of the way there. Then you just need to recognize a handful of answer words: links, rechts, geradeaus.
lessons.explain: To ask where something is: 'Wo ist...?' (Where is...?) — e.g. 'Wo ist der Bahnhof?'. To ask how to get somewhere: 'Wie komme ich zum/zur...?' (How do I get to the...?). The answers use direction words: links (left), rechts (right), geradeaus (straight ahead). A polite opener helps: 'Entschuldigung' (Excuse me) before you ask. You won't catch every word of the reply — that's fine. Listen for links / rechts / geradeaus and a landmark, and you'll find your way.

examples.english: Excuse me, where is the train station?

examples.german: Gehen Sie geradeaus und dann links.
examples.english: Go straight ahead and then left.
How do I get to the center?

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Excuse me, where is the toilet?
vocabulary.memoryHook: -ung → die. Say it first to politely stop someone.

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: The bank is on the left.
The supermarket is on the right.

vocabulary.memoryHook: die Toilette — possibly the most urgent phrase to know abroad.

exercises.prompt: How do you politely ask 'Where is the train station?'
exercises.explanation: 'Entschuldigung' (excuse me) opens politely, and 'wo ist...?' asks where something is. 'Wo' is the key direction-asking word.

exercises.prompt: Someone says 'Gehen Sie rechts.' Which way do you go?
exercises.hint: rechts and 'right' both start with r.
exercises.explanation: rechts = right. Remember the pairs: links = left (l/l), rechts = right (r/r), geradeaus = straight ahead.
geradeaus = straight ahead. It combines gerade (straight) + aus. This is the word to listen for when the answer is simply 'keep going'.
